claims.claim: Government Surveillance Powers Expanded Without Judicial Oversight
claims.source: Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743, gazetted 7 February 2025, official AGC/MCMC copies: https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/2669326_BI/A1743%20BI-%20COMMUNICATIONS%20AND%20MULTIMEDIA%20(AMENDMENT)%20ACT%202025.pdf and https://www.mcmc.gov.my/skmmgovmy/media/General/pdf2/A1743-BI-COMMUNICATIONS-AND-MULTIMEDIA-AMENDMENT-ACT-2025-1.pdf; Federal Gazette commencement notice P.U.(B) 61/2025: https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputp/2685090/PUB%2061.pdf
claims.correction: The CMA amendment did expand certain interception, retention, preservation, disclosure and record-keeping powers without adding a judicial warrant requirement. However, the headline overstates the issue because the most directly relevant new communications-data preservation/disclosure provisions in section 112 were not brought into force on 11 February 2025, and I found no primary source showing a recent Official Secrets Act surveillance amendment.
claims.stale_check: As of 17 May 2026, I found sources saying section 112 would commence later; I did not find a later official commencement notice in this search.

claims.claim: Amendments to Official Secrets Act and CMA expanded government electronic surveillance powers without requiring judicial warrants.
claims.source: Official Secrets Act 1972 Act 88 reprint incorporating amendments up to 1 January 2006, Commissioner of Law Revision/Percetakan Nasional Malaysia Bhd copy mirrored at https://legal.airforce.mil.my/images/lawofmalaysia/OFFICIAL%20SECRETS%20ACT%201972.pdf; Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743, official AGC/MCMC URLs above.
claims.correction: Replace with: The Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025 amended CMA section 252 and added sections 252A/252B and 268A, but I found no primary source for a recent Official Secrets Act amendment expanding electronic surveillance powers.
claims.stale_check: No evidence found of a recent OSA amendment before or after the analysis date.

claims.claim: Surveillance powers expanded without judicial warrant requirements
claims.source: Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743, sections 111, 112 and 119; Federal Gazette P.U.(B) 61/2025 commencement notice excluding sections 92 and 112 from 11 February 2025 commencement.
claims.correction: The CMA amendments expand powers related to interception/retention and communications data, but section 112 on preservation/disclosure of communications data was not yet in force under the commencement notice. The statement should specify which powers are in force and avoid implying all newly added data powers are already operative.
claims.stale_check: Possible stale issue: if section 112 commenced after 11 February 2025, this would need updating. I did not verify a later commencement notice.

claims.claim: Amendments allow interception of communications for national security
claims.source: Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743, section 111 amending CMA section 252; Explanatory Statement clause 86 says the amendment is to be consistent with Criminal Procedure Code interception powers.
claims.correction: The CMA section 252 test is tied to investigations of offences under the CMA or its subsidiary legislation, not a general national-security interception power. National-security interception powers are found in other laws such as SOSMA, but that is not what this CMA amendment says.

claims.claim: Home Minister can authorize surveillance without court approval
claims.source: Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743, section 111: amended CMA section 252 refers to the Public Prosecutor authorising interception/retention; section 119: new section 268A empowers the Minister to make communications-data record-keeping rules, not to authorise individual surveillance.
claims.correction: Replace Home Minister with Public Prosecutor for section 252 interception/retention authorisation. The Minister's role under new section 268A is rule-making on retention of communications data, not case-by-case surveillance authorisation.

claims.claim: Previous framework required AG approval which served as limited check
claims.source: Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 section 252 as described in official amendment text; Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025 section 111 preserves and expands the Public Prosecutor authorisation framework. The Public Prosecutor is the Attorney General under Malaysia's constitutional/prosecutorial framework.
claims.correction: Use the legally precise term Public Prosecutor rather than AG approval. The claim should also clarify this was executive/prosecutorial approval, not judicial approval.

claims.claim: SUHAKAM documented 280 complaints of suspected surveillance in 2025
claims.source: SUHAKAM media statements searched, including Media Statement No. 06-2025 dated 18 January 2025 on mobile phone inspections at roadblocks: https://suhakam.org.my/2025/01/media-statement-no-06-2025_suhakam-calls-for-clarity-on-mobile-phone-inspections-at-roadblocks/
claims.correction: I could not find a SUHAKAM primary source documenting 280 suspected-surveillance complaints in 2025. Remove the number unless a SUHAKAM annual report, complaint dataset, or statement can be produced.
claims.stale_check: The analysis is dated 17 May 2026, so a full 2025 SUHAKAM annual report might exist or be forthcoming; I could not verify this figure within the search performed.

claims.claim: Journalists activists and opposition politicians reported phone monitoring
claims.source: No primary source found. SUHAKAM Media Statement No. 06-2025 discusses concerns about police inspection of mobile phones at roadblocks, not documented phone monitoring reports by journalists, activists and opposition politicians.
claims.correction: Either cite specific first-person complaints, court filings, SUHAKAM complaint records, or official investigations, or soften/remove the claim. Do not present it as documented fact without primary evidence.

claims.claim: Bar Council challenged amendments as violating Article 5 liberty protections
claims.source: Federal Constitution of Malaysia Article 5, official JAC PDF: https://jac.gov.my/sites/default/files/documents/Federal%20Constitution.pdf; Malaysian Bar AGM materials and resolutions found did discuss CMA surveillance concerns but I found no primary court filing challenging the 2025 amendments under Article 5.
claims.correction: I found support only for the existence of Article 5 liberty protections and past Bar concern about surveillance laws, not for a Bar Council legal challenge to these amendments. Remove or replace with a properly sourced statement if a filing exists.

claims.claim: Constitutional court hearing scheduled for Q3 2026
claims.source: Searches for Bar Council, Article 5, CMA, Official Secrets Act, surveillance and Q3 2026 hearing produced no primary court-listing or filing. Malaysia's superior courts hear constitutional issues; there is no separately named Constitutional Court in the Malaysian federal court structure.
claims.correction: Remove this claim unless a specific High Court, Court of Appeal or Federal Court case number and hearing notice can be produced. Avoid the phrase Constitutional court unless referring generically to a court hearing constitutional issues.

claims.claim: Surveillance expansion tests whether security powers can expand without proportional oversight
claims.source: Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743; Federal Constitution Article 5, official JAC PDF.
claims.correction: This is partly analytical, but the factual premise should be narrowed: the CMA amendment expands certain communications enforcement powers, not clearly security powers, and the proportionality claim requires legal analysis or a cited expert/court source.

claims.claim: Surveillance powers expanded without judicial oversight. Executive convenience does not justify bypassing constitutional checks.
claims.source: Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743; Federal Constitution Article 5, official JAC PDF.
claims.correction: The first sentence is partly supported for CMA section 252-style executive/prosecutorial authorisation, but 'bypassing constitutional checks' is a legal conclusion not verified by a court ruling. Recast as an argument, not a factual finding.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: SUHAKAM documented 280 complaints of suspected surveillance in 2025
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Specific number, year and institution could not be traced to a SUHAKAM primary source. The SUHAKAM source found concerned phone inspections at roadblocks and did not contain the 280 figure.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Bar Council challenged amendments as violating Article 5 liberty protections Constitutional court hearing scheduled for Q3 2026
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary court filing, court schedule, Malaysian Bar statement or case number was found. The reference to a Constitutional court is also suspect in Malaysia's court structure.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Federal Constitution Article 5, official JAC PDF: https://jac.gov.my/sites/default/files/documents/Federal%20Constitution.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Home Minister can authorize surveillance without court approval
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The CMA amendment text identifies the Public Prosecutor as the authorising actor for interception/retention under section 252. The Minister may make record-keeping rules under section 268A, which is not the same as authorising surveillance.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743, sections 111 and 119: https://www.mcmc.gov.my/skmmgovmy/media/General/pdf2/A1743-BI-COMMUNICATIONS-AND-MULTIMEDIA-AMENDMENT-ACT-2025-1.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Amendments to Official Secrets Act and CMA expanded government electronic surveillance powers without requiring judicial warrants.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The CMA portion is partly supportable, but the Official Secrets Act portion was not verified, and the frame omits that the new CMA section 112 communications-data provisions were excluded from the 11 February 2025 commencement notice.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Amendments allow interception of communications for national security
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The CMA amendment text ties section 252 interception/retention to investigations of offences under the CMA or its subsidiary legislation, not a general national-security power.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743, section 111: https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/2669326_BI/A1743%20BI-%20COMMUNICATIONS%20AND%20MULTIMEDIA%20(AMENDMENT)%20ACT%202025.pdf

omitted_facts: The Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025 was brought into force on 11 February 2025 except for sections 92 and 112; section 112 is the provision adding sections 252A and 252B on preservation and disclosure of communications data. Source: Federal Gazette P.U.(B) 61/2025, https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputp/2685090/PUB%2061.pdf

The amended CMA section 252 authorising actor is the Public Prosecutor, not the Home Minister. Source: Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743, section 111, https://www.mcmc.gov.my/skmmgovmy/media/General/pdf2/A1743-BI-COMMUNICATIONS-AND-MULTIMEDIA-AMENDMENT-ACT-2025-1.pdf

New CMA sections 252A and 252B concern preservation and disclosure of communications data by police or authorised officers; they are not framed as general content interception powers. Source: Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Act 2025, Act A1743, section 112, official AGC/MCMC URLs above.

The Official Secrets Act 1972 reprint found incorporates amendments only up to 1 January 2006; I found no primary source for a 2025 OSA surveillance amendment. Source: Official Secrets Act 1972 Act 88 reprint, https://legal.airforce.mil.my/images/lawofmalaysia/OFFICIAL%20SECRETS%20ACT%201972.pdf

SUHAKAM did issue a 18 January 2025 statement on mobile phone inspections at roadblocks, stating searches cannot be arbitrary and must be tied to legal search/investigation grounds, but that statement does not document 280 suspected-surveillance complaints. Source: https://suhakam.org.my/2025/01/media-statement-no-06-2025_suhakam-calls-for-clarity-on-mobile-phone-inspections-at-roadblocks/
source_assessment: The original analysis cites only two prior T4A issues, which are circular and not primary sources. It cites no statutes, gazettes, court filings, SUHAKAM reports, Malaysian Bar statements, parliamentary bills, or court listings. Institutional diversity is effectively absent: no government legal texts, no regulator materials, no rights-body primary records, no legal filings, no affected-party testimony, and no government response. The source-diversity estimate is therefore very low.
overall_assessment: The analysis is factually unreliable. It appears to mix a real CMA amendment concern with unsupported or wrong details: the Home Minister is misidentified as the surveillance authoriser, the Official Secrets Act amendment claim is not verified, the SUHAKAM 280-complaint figure is unsupported, and the Bar Council/Q3 2026 constitutional hearing claim is not verified. The broad concern about non-judicial surveillance oversight has a partial basis in CMA section 252, but the cards need major correction before publication.
